stem: A 60-foot flagpole stands vertically on level ground. When the sun is at a 53-degree angle of elevation, the flagpole casts a shadow on the ground. Given that sin 53° ≈ 4/5, cos 53° ≈ 3/5, and tan 53° ≈ 4/3, what is the length of the shadow, in feet?
explanation: The flagpole height (60 feet) is the side opposite the 53° angle of elevation, and the shadow length is the side adjacent to the angle. Using tangent: tan 53° = opposite/adjacent = 60/shadow. Solving for the shadow: shadow = 60 ÷ tan 53° = 60 ÷ (4/3) = 60 × (3/4) = 45 feet.

distractorRationale.A: Treated the flagpole height as the hypotenuse and multiplied by cos 53° instead of dividing by tan 53°: 60 × cos 53° = 60 × (3/5) = 36.
distractorRationale.C: Treated the flagpole height as the hypotenuse and multiplied by sin 53° instead of dividing by tan 53°: 60 × sin 53° = 60 × (4/5) = 48.
distractorRationale.D: Multiplied by tan 53° instead of dividing, inverting the tangent operation: 60 × tan 53° = 60 × (4/3) = 80.

imageDescription: A right triangle showing a vertical flagpole labeled 60 ft on the left side. The horizontal ground to the right of the flagpole base represents the shadow, labeled with a question mark. A dashed line from the tip of the shadow to the top of the flagpole represents a sun ray, and the angle between this line and the ground is labeled 53°.
